title: The Professional Workflow
subtitle: How developers actually use git

sections.content: Now you know all the git commands. Here's how professional developers use them together every day:
keyPoints: 2. Create a branch: git checkout -b feature-name
7. Create a Pull Request on GitHub
sections.analogy: The professional git workflow is like a team relay race. Each runner (developer) takes the baton (pulls latest code), runs their leg (works on a branch), passes it forward (pushes + creates a PR), and the coach (reviewer) gives the OK before the next handoff.

sections.content: **Commit messages matter.** Your future self (and teammates) will read these. A good commit message explains WHAT changed and WHY, not just that something changed.
keyPoints: Good: 'Fix login button not responding on mobile'
Good: 'Add search feature to product list'
Good: 'Update API endpoint for user authentication'

sections.question: What's the first thing you should do when starting work on a new feature?
sections.explanation: Always branch first — it keeps main clean. If your experiment doesn't work out, you can delete the branch without any damage to the main codebase.

completionMessage: You now understand how professional developers work with git every day. The workflow is: pull, branch, code, add, commit, push, review, merge. Next: practice writing good commits.
